source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Browsers break lines greedily by default — they fill each line as far as it goes, which leaves headlines lopsided and paragraphs ending on a single orphaned word. Two CSS properties fix this for free: `text-wrap: balance` distributes a short run of text evenly across its lines (made for titles), and `text-wrap: pretty` avoids orphans at the end of longer paragraphs.

blocks.caption: Drag the width and switch modes — watch how the lines redistribute.

blocks.text: Rule of thumb: **balance** for anything under ~4 lines, **pretty** for body copy. Both are progressive enhancements — old browsers simply ignore them.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: On macOS the default subpixel rendering makes text look slightly bolder and fuzzier than designed. Switching to grayscale antialiasing renders type thinner and crisper — closer to what you see in Figma. Apply it once, globally.

blocks.caption: The difference is subtle at body sizes and obvious on light text over dark.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Most fonts give digits proportional widths — a 1 is narrower than an 8 — so any number that updates in place (timers, counters, prices) jitters sideways as it ticks. `font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums` makes every digit the same width, and the layout holds still.

blocks.caption: Same timer, twice. Only one of them vibrates.

blocks.text: Check your typeface first — some fonts (Inter, for one) draw tabular digits differently enough that you'll want to compare before shipping.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: When an icon swaps to reflect state — copy turning into a checkmark — an instant swap reads as a glitch. Cross-fading the two icons with a touch of scale and blur makes the state change feel acknowledged rather than replaced.

blocks.caption: Copy something. The left button swaps instantly; the right one transitions.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: CSS transitions retarget mid-flight: change the destination while one is running and it glides toward the new value from wherever it is. Keyframe animations don't — they run their fixed timeline to the end, and re-triggering one snaps it back to frame zero. For anything the user can poke repeatedly, that difference is the difference between an interface that feels responsive and one that feels stuck.

blocks.caption: Spam the toggle. The transition follows you; the keyframes ignore you.

blocks.text: Transitions for interactions, keyframes for one-off sequences (entrances, loaders). If a user can re-trigger it, it must be interruptible.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Animating a whole page in as one block is heavy-handed. Split the content into pieces and let them enter a beat apart — opacity, a small translateY, a hint of blur — and the same entrance suddenly has rhythm. Delay each piece by ~80–100ms more than the last.

blocks.caption: One entrance, three granularities. Replay each.

blocks.text: Set `--stagger: 1..n` on successive elements. Keep the count modest — staggering forty list items keeps the user waiting for no reason.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Entrances introduce something the user should notice; exits remove something they're already done with. Giving both the same weight makes leaving feel as loud as arriving. Let exits step back — a quick fade with a touch of blur — instead of sliding dramatically off-screen.

blocks.caption: Dismiss items in both lists. The right one lets go quietly.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: When a rounded element sits inside another rounded element, reusing the same radius makes the corners visually diverge — the gap between the two curves is thicker at the corner than along the edges. The fix is one formula: the outer radius should equal the inner radius plus the padding between them. Then the curves share a center and the spacing reads even all the way around.

blocks.caption: Scrub the values. The naive version pinches at the corners.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Perfectly centered is not always perceived as centered. A play triangle sitting mathematically in the middle of a circle looks like it's drifting left, because its visual mass sits toward its back edge. Asymmetric shapes need a nudge — a pixel or two of margin against the math — until they *look* right. Fixing it inside the SVG itself is cleanest, so every use of the icon inherits the correction.

blocks.caption: Toggle the correction. Trust your eye, not the ruler.

blocks.text: Same story for text next to icons, chevrons in buttons, and badges beside labels: when it looks off but measures fine, the measurement is what's wrong.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: A 1px solid border is a flat, opaque line — it sits on top of whatever's behind it and always looks the same. A ring built from layered box-shadows is translucent: it picks up the background, so the same card works on white, on tint, on a pattern. Stack a hairline ring with one or two soft drops and you get an edge plus depth in a single declaration.

blocks.caption: Border vs shadow ring, on quiet and busy backgrounds. Hover the cards.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: Photos with light edges — skies, white studio backgrounds — bleed into a light page and look like they're floating unbounded. A 1px inset outline at 10% black quietly contains every image, whatever its content, and disappears on images that never needed it.

blocks.caption: Toggle the outline. The light-edged image is the one it saves.

source.author: Jakub Krehel
source.article: Details That Make Interfaces Feel Better

blocks.text: None of these details matters much alone — each is a percent or two of polish. Applied together, the same component reads a class apart: balanced text, concentric corners, a shadow ring instead of a border, tabular digits in the timer, an icon that transitions when tapped, everything aligned to the eye.

blocks.caption: The same card with everything off, then everything on.

blocks.text: Craft is cumulative. Users can't name a single one of these details — they just say the interface feels better.
